question: What was the accuracy of your best performing model?
answer: The QAT FFNN model achieved 99.74% accuracy. [Passage 1] The proposed TinyTCN model achieves the highest performance after fine-tuning. [Passage 2] The proposed TinyTCN DL model achieves the best overall performance. [Passage 3] The proposed TinyTCN model achieves 0.9748 accuracy in early fusion and 0.9772 accuracy in late fusion when fine-tuned on the Test Anxiety dataset and converted to TinyML. [Passage 2] The proposed TinyTCN model achieves 0.9900 accuracy in both early and late fusion when fine-tuned on the Test Anxiety dataset. [Passage 3]

question: What neural architecture did you use for stress detection?
answer:
The paper proposes a novel QAT FFNN model, a lightweight Feed-Forward Neural Network optimised using tiny machine learning techniques such as Quantisation Aware Training and Pruning. [Passage 2]

The paper also presents an in-depth comparative analysis of both early and late fusion methods for multimodal data fusion at multiple optimisation levels. Baseline machine learning (ML) models and TinyTCN trained on the WESAD dataset depict that late-fusion (ensemble) approaches initially outperform early fusion in terms of accuracy and F1 score. However, after transfer learning using the real-world Test Anxiety Dataset and subsequent TinyML optimisation, including quantisation, distillation, and TFLite Micro compilation, the proposed TinyTCN, along with other ML models with early fusion, maintains performance comparable to their late-fusion counterparts. [Passage 3]
